item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crazed reality bender punishing 'bad' parents by turning them into soup and feeding them to unknowing homeless people. Honestly if they genuinely were crappy parents I think this chap may be killing two birds with one stone! Sadly I rather doubt he is actually correct about the parents… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From the interview, the Chef apparently targets single parents that don't meet his standards of a good role model.
Parents show up later on the menu at soup kitchens but kids and Chef haven't been found yet.
The Chef was seen talking and singing to his soup. Like a caregiver would interact with a small child?

Taken all together, I think our broth-based reality bender is adopting the kids in his own twisted way. The parents go to the soup line, so they can do at least some good after they "failed" as parents. But he keeps the kids, as soup, with him. Either a bunch of bowls scattered around his squat, or "together forever" by being slurped up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what I found "creepy" about this SCP was the interview log…the soup described a homeless guy talking and singing to soup and then he woke up as soup….

the creepiest part about all of this is, somewhere, there is a guy who has the ability to turn people into soup….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m left confused as to how the Foundation knows all 12 11 cans are haunted if they only spoke to one.

Intriguing SCProcedures gives way to an interesting description, with just enough detail to stand out and keep me invested. It then segues quickly into a disturbing narrative thread left disturbingly unsettled by the interviews conclusion. +1, and congrats on the triple digits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Author here.

I meant bubbling as in "letters rising so quickly they caused bubbling", not boiling.

As for the explanation: it's a bowl of soup that somehow functions as a neural network despite a complete lack of morphological structure, is capable of not only generating free energy but maintaining thermal homeostasis, and somehow translating neural activity into spontaneous generation of matter (e.g. edible letter-shaped pasta). I do not believe science comes into any of this, hence why it's an anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So you're basically saying that I shouldn't be worried about the movement of the letters so much as the fact that they are appearing and disappearing in the soup, which should unleash a lot of energy (but doesn't)? And the reason that it doesn't release the energy is because the soups happen to… already defy thermodynamics?

I would accept that as a really good explanation, but I don't know if the letters appear or if they are already in the soup and they just happen to rise to the surface. The wording of that part in the article makes it seem as though they are already in the soup and that they are rising to the surface quickly enough to make it boil.

Here's another thought: what if the author meant 'boil' in the way that means the soup is bubbling, bot not literally boiling? The same way that perfectly average bubbles rise in water?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anomolous manifestation of letters isn't equivalent to stirring though. Note that the system already appears to violate thermodynamics by maintaining a constant temperature despite no apparent work being done on the system. If the matter in the letters spontaneously appears and disappears this could easily be adding energy to the system (which again isn't behaving properly to begin with) without causing significant enough movement of the fluid to cause cavitation. Since both the energy that is keeping it from thermal equilibrium with the environment and the energy required to make letters is external to the system, any energy released by compression of the fluid is going to be energy added to the system, as well as any energy released by disintegration of the letters (up to a whole lot of energy; say a single letter has a mass of 2 grams it would release 180 TJ into the soup if it is all retained; or the equivalent of a 43 kt warhead!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://what-if.xkcd.com/71/

I'd just like to make a mention that "(Note: Letters begin to appear on the surface quickly enough for SCP-1354-1 to boil.)" is, um, well, refer to the XKCD What-If article.

I would like to mention that I believe there used to be an extension to the article that showed what would happen if you ignored fluid dynamics. It wasn't pretty, and it involved some serious radiation and fission.

So, in closing, if those letters are moving quickly enough for the soup to boil… the amount of energy released/needed might cause nuclear fission, though someone with a physics degree or whatnot would have to check me on that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bit of a necropost, but SCP-2013.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I'd be pretty thoroughly "meh" about this one, except that the completely unresolved bit about the missing kids is freaking me out big time. Something's happening to them and I can't for the life of me figure out what - somehow I've almost got the impression that the soupified parents could be incidental to what's really going on, and the kids are the real targets.

+1 I guess, this is going to bug me all day and not in a "poorly written" kind of way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was me. I was in the process of reworking the SCP with some help, however I hadn't come around to finish the article when this one popped up.
I like this version. Does give me that nice 'what-the-heck' sensation. :) +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Hey, Brian! There's a mysterious message in my Alpha-Bits…!
"…it says, 'OOOOOOOO'!"

"Peter… those are Cheerios…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's an amazing image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With a spoon, I'd think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how whatever it is targets single parents. That's a really scary thought, just because it's hunting down these everyday people with a special everyday struggle of taking care of their kid on their own. It's specifically targeting decent people.

One thing I don't get: How do you interrupt alphabet soup?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How much energy does a bowl of soup give off?
It goes from 37 to 100 C in a minute, HOW CAN WE HARVEST THIS HEAT ENERGY.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was good. I would like to hear more about the homeless guy, but it kind of adds to it to know very little about him, honestly. And it somehow makes you feel for a can of soup. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That wasn't me, FYI. This is my first soup-based SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking organisms. You're right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's worse than that. The soup itself is alive. And I don't just mean the twelve eleven samples of soup in the Foundation's possession. I mean all soup is alive, and always has been. For millenia we've been eating soup, oblivious to the cries of horror and agony just outside our hearing range. We are responsible for the massacre of millions, perhaps billions of bowls of soup.

But the game has changed. A deliverer has arisen, a bowl of soup with a unique power: The power to swap bodies with humans. This savior stole the body of a man, and left the man's soul screaming silently, trapped forever in a bowl of noodles and broth. And now it roams the land, disguised as one of us, teaching other bowls of soup how to steal the bodies of humans. And it is raising an army. At first it preyed on the homeless, the destitute, anybody who wouldn't be missed. But with its capture by the Foundation, it now has the chance to infiltrate the Foundation from within. Soon the takeover of the Overwatch will be complete, and the full might of the Foundation will belong to the deliverer.

The hour of the Soup Messiah is at hand. And humanity will drown in the boiling broth of His rage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty sure it's exothermic, as it releases more heat than it absorbs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Additionally, instances of SCP-1354 are exothermic, maintain a constant temperature of 37 degrees celsius and have shown no signs of evaporation.

Shouldn't that be "endothermic"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably the sweet spot of WTF; well-executed. Weird, but written so that I keep reading, and then sorta explains the weird a little. Upvoted with a sad smile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Guys, if I'm ever soup and you're, like… lost in the wilderness, I give you permission to eat me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: YES! The talking soup is back! I already loved the idea the first time (though execution was really bad) and now I can't deny you my upvote. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much improved from the last version. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I chuckled, but it's not bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly what I was thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, i found imagine it hard to perform cannibalism on a living human, as we don't possess the needed strength for surviving the severing of limbs without using proper instruments. The "and" is not special from that point.

don't worry it is just a funny typo I let stay there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A homeless reality bender who talks to and/or turns people into food?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So does that make us murderers and cannibals?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did anyone notice we apparently ate one of the sapient talking soup people?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True. With a human subject, they might close with a "subject became too agitated and researcher ended interview to avoid a physical altercation" but that probably doesn't apply with soup… I could see the Foundation refusing to talk about the daughter, since it's irrelevant, but they probably should poke a bit more into the homeless guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, in an endearing sort of way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point on that, actually. I tweaked it a bit to say, "…brought to Foundation attention after persistent rumors of 'talking soup'."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In a world with several thousand anomalous objects to contain, they probably would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We've already got psychic soup, and this article doesn't really do anything for me.

Also;

SCP-1354 was brought to Foundation attention after rumors of “talking soup” began circulating in local homeless shelters and soup kitchens.

When I worked across the street from a homeless shelter, I heard a rumor that Obama was a robot from outer space. Maybe the Foundation should investigate that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmm, it's okay. Better than the previous one. Needs more work. I'd also recommend not using a collapsible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is better than the recent oracular alphabet soup that bombed, but I'm somewhat on the fence about this one. I really do think that the researcher wouldn't have cut off the interview at that point, but would have instead tried to get more info about the homeless guy.
